text: The surgeon begins the procedure for a left indirect inguinal hernia by creating space laterally and working anteriorly to take down the peritoneal flap. The medial umbilical ligament is cauterized, and the peritoneal flap is manipulated, revealing the medial and lateral leaflets. The surgeon carefully thins the flap medially and laterally, pushing fat laterally to protect the nerves. The dissection progresses towards the deep internal ring.

The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessels positioned to the right and begins to visualize the iliopubic tract, which runs from the anterior superior iliac spine to the pubic tubercle. The surgeon works on dissecting a large cord lipoma, attempting to separate it from the hernia sac, while carefully applying cautery to avoid nerve entrapment along the iliopubic tract.

The surgeon uses traction and counter-traction techniques to separate the hernia sac, revealing the spermatic vessels and vas deferens beneath. The edge of the hernia sac is visible as the surgeon carefully manipulates it away from surrounding structures. The cord lipoma is truncated, allowing lateral fat to cover the nerves, before being removed.

The surgeon continues to dissect the hernia sac, creating space around the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament. The dissection is performed with precision to avoid hitting the corona mortis while preparing for needle placement.

The surgeon carefully touches up around the iliac vein, maintaining a lateral plane. An extra-large mesh is set for the indirect inguinal hernia, and suturing begins on the side of the hernia to ensure high lateral fixation, allowing the mesh to remain deep in the pocket.
The surgeon performs a high lateral fixation of the mesh, avoiding fixation in areas obscured by fat. The Cooper's ligament is fixated, and the flap is closed to prevent mesh migration.
